network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-supported prebiotic routes to nicotinamide centered on two experimentally established convergent heterocycle-forming strategies: (i) the Orgel/Trapp C3+C3 route through cyanoacetaldehyde, propiolaldehyde, 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde, and nicotinonitrile; and (ii) the Cleaves-Miller biomimetic aspartate plus DHAP route through quinolinic acid to nicotinic acid, with the direct asparagine branch to nicotinamide. Surface cyanosulfidic chemistry supplies cyanoacetaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, glyceraldehyde, ribose, and phosphorylated carbohydrate precursors; hydrothermal chemistry supplies formate, acetate, pyruvate, and amino-acid precursor pools that connect to aspartate/asparagine formation; biochemical/prebiotic steps assemble ribosides and nucleotides. Ten distinct pathways are enumerated by combining these validated cores with different feeder and downstream branches, while preserving the critical literature conditions for Kim-Benner ribosylation, Bechtel-Kurrle-Trapp NMN/NAD+ assembly, and Cleaves-Miller quinolinate chemistry. The network is designed so that nicotinamide is reached directly by nicotinonitrile hydrolysis or by the asparagine-dependent nonenzymatic NAD biosynthesis analogue, while also showing that ribosylated/nucleotide forms are functionally important intermediates rather than simple degradative dead ends.

molecules.iupac_name: adenosine monophosphate activated by isothiatriazine phosphate chemistry

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, H2-rich, anoxic

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated reduction of CO2 by H2 to a one-carbon reduced carboxylate pool.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S minerals are established catalysts for CO2 reduction to formate and related C1 products in alkaline vent analogues, providing a realistic reduced-carbon feedstock that connects vent chemistry to later surface synthesis (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to acetate

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal vent, H2-rich, pH gradient

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction and C-C bond formation from reduced C1 species on Fe-S/oxide surfaces yields acetate-like products.
reactions.reasoning: Acetate formation under hydrothermal mineral catalysis is consistent with Fe-S world and vent carbon-fixation analog chemistry and is included here only as a literature-supported environmental connector to broader organosynthesis (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from CO2 and H2

conditions.other: anoxic subseafloor hydrothermal conditions

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-promoted multicarbon fixation generates alpha-keto acid products including pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate and related alpha-keto acids can arise under hydrothermal mineral catalysis and provide a plausible feeder pool for prebiotic amino-acid synthesis, linking vent geochemistry to the aspartate/asparagine branch required by Cleaves-Miller chemistry (Muchowska et al., 2019; Varma et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Reductive amination and carboxylation to aspartate pool

conditions.other: nonenzymatic amino-acid pool generation at mineral-water interfaces

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid amination/carboxylation sequence expands hydrothermal keto-acid pools into dicarboxylic amino acids.
reactions.reasoning: This step is a necessary connector rather than a directly demonstrated single-step literature transformation to nicotinamide; it is included conservatively to bridge validated hydrothermal keto-acid chemistry to the experimentally supported aspartate-dependent quinolinate route. Aspartate itself is the substrate used in the nonenzymatic NAD biosynthesis analogue (Cleaves & Miller, 2001), and pyruvate/amino-acid network expansion is supported by prebiotic keto-acid chemistry (Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Aspartate amidation to asparagine pool

conditions.other: condensation in concentrated prebiotic pools; included as network connector to the Cleaves-Miller asparagine branch

reactions.mechanism: Amidation of the side-chain carboxylate generates the amide-bearing substrate analogous to the direct nicotinamide-forming branch.
reactions.reasoning: Cleaves and Miller explicitly reported that replacing aspartate with asparagine in the nonenzymatic pathway produces nicotinamide directly. This reaction is included only to connect broader prebiotic amino-acid pools to that literature-supported branch; the amidation itself remains more speculative than the downstream conversion (Cleaves & Miller, 2001).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic formation of cyanoacetylene from HCN feedstock

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system on sulfide-rich mineral surfaces
conditions.other: UV-irradiated cyanosulfidic network, phosphate-buffered near pH 7

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived photoredox network generates higher nitriles including cyanoacetylene under Cu-mediated sulfide chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. described cyanosulfidic protometabolism furnishing the feedstocks required for nucleotide and heterocycle synthesis; cyanoacetylene is generated in this framework and is the direct precursor to cyanoacetaldehyde by hydration. Mineral agents reflect the required sulfide-rich photoredox setting specified in the network instructions (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydration of cyanoacetylene to cyanoacetaldehyde

conditions.other: water-rich surface setting

reactions.mechanism: Cyanoacetylene hydrates to the beta-oxo nitrile cyanoacetaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanoacetaldehyde is a key nexus molecule in nicotinamide chemistry and is formed by hydration of cyanoacetylene in the literature summary connecting Sutherland-type networks to Orgel/Trapp pyridine synthesis (Patel et al., 2015; Dowler et al., 1970; Bechtel et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Electric discharge generation of propiolaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral-rich evaporitic/surficial collection after electric discharge production
conditions.other: volcanic lightning/electric discharge atmosphere, followed by condensation into surface reservoirs

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge chemistry generates propiolaldehyde, which is trapped in surface waters or evaporitic pools.
reactions.reasoning: Dowler et al. used propiolaldehyde produced by electric discharge on CH4/H2O mixtures as a validated precursor for nicotinonitrile formation. This step is represented as atmospheric production feeding surface collection, with mineral surfaces concentrating the product rather than serving as the primary energetic driver (Dowler et al., 1970).

reactions.name: Condensation of cyanoacetaldehyde and propiolaldehyde to 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde

conditions.other: ammonia-bearing, concentrated surficial setting

reactions.mechanism: A C3+C3 condensation generates the linear dialdehyde nitrile branch-point intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: The Dowler-Orgel route identifies 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde as the crucial linear intermediate formed from propiolaldehyde and cyanoacetaldehyde before pyridine ring closure; Bechtel et al. later used the same branch-point in full NAD+ assembly (Dowler et al., 1970; Bechtel et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Chichibabin-type cyclization of 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde to nicotinonitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral surface concentration under anoxic surficial conditions
conditions.other: ammonia must be available; evaporative concentration improves yield

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia-promoted Chichibabin-type ring closure of the linear dialdehyde nitrile forms 3-cyanopyridine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the core Orgel heterocycle-forming step. Dowler et al. showed that the dialdehyde intermediate cyclizes with ammonia to nicotinonitrile; regioselective access to the 3-isomer is central to the nicotinamide problem (Dowler et al., 1970).

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis of nicotinonitrile to nicotinamide

conditions.other: controlled hydration; partial nitrile hydrolysis favored over full oxidation

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration/hydrolysis gives the carboxamide without proceeding fully to the acid.
reactions.reasoning: Dowler et al. explicitly reported that nicotinonitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis to nicotinamide on the way to nicotinic acid; this is the most direct literature-supported route to the target heterocycle (Dowler et al., 1970).

reactions.name: Complete hydrolysis of nicotinonitrile to nicotinic acid

conditions.other: further hydration beyond the amide stage

reactions.mechanism: Extended nitrile hydrolysis converts the carboxamide-equivalent intermediate to the free acid.
reactions.reasoning: The Orgel route yields nicotinic acid upon full hydrolysis of nicotinonitrile. This branch is included because nicotinic acid is a major experimentally and meteoritically validated reservoir of vitamin B3 chemistry and a convergence point with quinolinate chemistry (Dowler et al., 1970; Oba et al., 2023; Smith et al., 2014).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox formation of glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox on sulfide-rich mineral surfaces
conditions.other: UV irradiation, phosphate-buffered around pH 7, H2S present

reactions.mechanism: HCN-derived photoredox homologation furnishes the key two- and three-carbon sugar precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland demonstrated prebiotic photoredox generation of glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, and Patel et al. embedded this in a broader cyanosulfidic network. These molecules are the literature-supported carbohydrate feeders into ribose and phosphorylated substrate formation relevant to nicotinamide/NAD chemistry (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Borate-stabilized formose/aldol route to ribose-rich sugar pool

conditions.other: evaporitic pools with wet-dry cycling

reactions.mechanism: Aldol/formose chemistry expands lower sugars to pentoses, with borate stabilizing ribose-containing pools.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research report identifies glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde as sugar precursors and borate minerals as stabilizers of ribose for glycosylation chemistry. This step connects the cyanosulfidic carbohydrate network to both Trapp and Kim-Benner ribosylation branches (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012; Patel et al., 2015; Kim & Benner, 2018).

reactions.name: Ribose and ammonia condensation to ribosylamine

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized sugar pool on clay surfaces
conditions.other: ammonia must be premixed with ribose before aldehyde precursors are added

reactions.mechanism: Ammoniation of the open-chain sugar gives ribosylamine, enabling telescoped heterocycle formation with glycosylation.
reactions.reasoning: Bechtel et al. emphasized that ammonia must be pre-mixed with ribose to form ribosylamine before introduction of the dialdehyde precursor; otherwise only free nicotinonitrile forms. This ordering is critical and is preserved here (Bechtel et al., 2024).

reactions.name: One-pot formation of nicotinamide riboside via nicotinonitrile riboside intermediate

conditions.catalyst: surface concentration in mild aqueous medium
conditions.other: ribosylamine preformed; cyclization gives nicotinonitrile riboside followed by hydrolysis to nicotinamide riboside

reactions.mechanism: The dialdehyde branch-point reacts with ribosylamine so that ring closure and glycosylation occur together, producing a ribosylated cyanopyridine that hydrolyzes to nicotinamide riboside.
reactions.reasoning: This follows the key Trapp breakthrough: 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde cyclizes with ribosylamine directly to nicotinonitrile riboside, which then hydrolyzes to nicotinamide riboside. It is one of the strongest literature-supported bridges from heterocycle chemistry to nucleotide assembly (Bechtel et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Kim-Benner evaporative ribosylation to nicotinamide riboside 2'-phosphate

conditions.catalyst: Mg2+ or Ca2+ under evaporative conditions
conditions.other: 85C drying chemistry; cyclic phosphate blocks alpha-face and enforces beta-glycoside formation

reactions.mechanism: N-glycosylation of nicotinamide by ribose-1,2-cyclic phosphate yields the stabilized beta riboside 2'-phosphate regioisomer.
reactions.reasoning: Kim and Benner did not obtain free nicotinamide riboside; they obtained the 2'-phosphate product under evaporative 85C conditions with divalent cations. That exact product identity is preserved here to avoid the error noted in earlier failed attempts (Kim & Benner, 2018).

reactions.name: Regioselective 5'-phosphorylation of nicotinamide riboside to NMN

conditions.other: pH 6, 23C, 7 days, mild conditions; low yield around 2%

reactions.mechanism: Activated phosphate transfer selectively installs the 5'-phosphate on the labile riboside under unusually mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Bechtel et al. specifically used ITO-P at pH 6 and 23C for 7 days to achieve 5'-selective phosphorylation of nicotinamide riboside to NMN at about 2% yield. These exact literature-sensitive conditions are included because omission of ITO-P was a major flaw in prior attempts (Bechtel et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Photochemical pyrophosphate coupling of NMN to NAD+

conditions.other: 278 nm UV irradiation; photochemical coupling with light-activated adenylate

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven activation enables pyrophosphate bond formation between NMN and activated AMP.
reactions.reasoning: Bechtel et al. completed the first prebiotic NAD+ synthesis by coupling NMN with ITO-AMP under 278 nm UV light, drawing on Sutherland phosphorimidazolide activation chemistry. This step is included because the report emphasizes that the complete nucleotide is functionally more relevant than free nicotinamide (Bechtel et al., 2024; Mariani et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal mineral reduction of NAD+ to 1,4-NADH

conditions.catalyst: Ni-Fe alloy-like vent minerals represented within reduced iron/sulfide hydrothermal setting
conditions.other: pH 8.5; stereospecific reduction to 1,4-NADH in alkaline vent conditions

reactions.mechanism: Hydrogen-driven mineral-surface hydride transfer selectively reduces the nicotinamide ring in NAD+.
reactions.reasoning: Henriques Pereira et al. showed stereospecific reduction of NAD+ to 1,4-NADH on Ni-Fe minerals at 40C, pH 8.5, and 5 bar H2, while NMN was overreduced. This strongly supports the functional importance of the complete dinucleotide in hydrothermal settings. The mineral list is constrained by the schema, so reduced iron/sulfide hydrothermal agents are used to represent the vent context while citing the actual Ni-Fe result (Henriques Pereira et al., 2025).

reactions.name: Nonenzymatic aspartate plus DHAP pathway to quinolinic acid

conditions.other: phosphorylated substrates favored; phosphate enhances yields; nonenzymatic multi-step sequence

reactions.mechanism: A biomimetic analogue of the bacterial de novo NAD pathway converts aspartate and DHAP through several uncatalyzed intermediates to quinolinic acid.
reactions.reasoning: Cleaves and Miller demonstrated that the bacterial de novo pathway from aspartate and DHAP can proceed nonenzymatically through quinolinic acid, with phosphorylated substrates giving substantially higher yields. This is one of the two principal literature-validated routes to vitamin B3 chemistry (Cleaves & Miller, 2001).

reactions.name: Nonenzymatic conversion of quinolinic acid to nicotinic acid

conditions.other: continuation of the Cleaves-Miller nonenzymatic pathway

reactions.mechanism: Decarboxylative progression along the biomimetic NAD biosynthesis analogue furnishes nicotinic acid from quinolinate.
reactions.reasoning: Cleaves and Miller showed that the nonenzymatic pathway proceeds through quinolinic acid to nicotinic acid in yields up to about 7%, establishing nicotinic acid as a robust prebiotic convergence product (Cleaves & Miller, 2001).

reactions.name: Asparagine plus DHAP route to nicotinamide

conditions.other: phosphorylated carbohydrate substrate; direct amide-forming branch

reactions.mechanism: Substituting asparagine for aspartate in the nonenzymatic de novo pathway analogue preserves ring assembly while delivering the carboxamide product directly.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction strictly follows the literature correction requested by the failure analysis: Cleaves and Miller reported direct nicotinamide formation when asparagine replaced aspartate. No serine substitution is used here (Cleaves & Miller, 2001).

reactions.name: Phosphate-assisted formation of DHAP-like phosphorylated triose pool

conditions.other: phosphorylated triose generation/connectivity step included because Cleaves-Miller found phosphorylated substrates markedly superior

reactions.mechanism: Surface phosphorylation of a triose precursor furnishes the DHAP-class substrate required for the biomimetic quinolinate pathway.
reactions.reasoning: The report stresses that DHAP and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate were much more effective than nonphosphorylated analogues in the Cleaves-Miller system. This connector is included to ensure network continuity from the cyanosulfidic sugar pool to the validated phosphorylated-substrate pathway, while acknowledging that exact prebiotic DHAP synthesis remains an open systems-chemistry challenge (Cleaves & Miller, 2001; Patel et al., 2015).

pathways.name: Direct Orgel nicotinonitrile-to-nicotinamide pathway
pathways.description: Surface C3+C3 condensation gives the dialdehyde intermediate, which cyclizes with ammonia to nicotinonitrile and hydrolyzes partially to nicotinamide.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic sugars feeding Trapp ribosylamine branch to nicotinamide riboside and nicotinamide
pathways.description: HCN photoredox supplies sugars, ribose forms ribosylamine, and the Trapp one-pot heterocycle/glycosylation route passes through nicotinamide riboside, which is connected to the target pool through the shared nicotinamide node.

pathways.name: Kim-Benner nicotinamide ribosylation branch
pathways.description: Preformed nicotinamide reacts with ribose-1,2-cyclic phosphate under evaporative conditions to give the stabilized 2'-phosphate riboside derivative.

pathways.name: Trapp complete nucleotide assembly branch from the Orgel dialdehyde intermediate
pathways.description: The dialdehyde reacts with ribosylamine to give nicotinamide riboside, which is phosphorylated with ITO-P to NMN and can continue to NAD+.

pathways.name: Full prebiotic NAD+ assembly followed by hydrothermal redox activation
pathways.description: Surface synthesis builds NMN and NAD+, then hydrothermal mineral reduction generates 1,4-NADH, illustrating Hydrothermal ↔ Surface → Biochemical interconnection.

pathways.name: Cleaves-Miller aspartate-DHAP to quinolinate to nicotinic acid branch
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation feeds amino-acid pools; surface phosphorylation supplies DHAP; the nonenzymatic de novo NAD analogue yields quinolinate and nicotinic acid.

pathways.name: Direct Cleaves-Miller asparagine branch to nicotinamide
pathways.description: Aspartate is converted to asparagine, which with DHAP gives nicotinamide directly in the reported nonenzymatic pathway analogue.

pathways.name: Hybrid hydrothermal-surface route to nicotinamide via nicotinonitrile
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetate/formate production supports atmospheric/surface precursor availability, while the Orgel cyclization supplies nicotinamide through nicotinonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Convergent surface route to nicotinamide through shared dialdehyde hub
pathways.description: Cyanoacetaldehyde from cyanosulfidic chemistry and propiolaldehyde from discharge chemistry meet at 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde, then proceed to nicotinamide.

pathways.name: Alternative vitamin B3 convergence: nicotinic acid reservoir and nicotinamide direct branch
pathways.description: The network shows two parallel vitamin B3 outcomes from distinct routes: nicotinic acid via quinolinate or full nitrile hydrolysis, and nicotinamide directly via either nicotinonitrile partial hydrolysis or the asparagine-DHAP branch.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the strongest direct route to nicotinamide and matches the classic Orgel/Trapp literature core well. The condensation of cyanoacetaldehyde with propiolaldehyde to 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde, followed by ammonia-promoted cyclization to nicotinonitrile and partial hydrolysis to nicotinamide, is highly literature-aligned. Main weakness is the underspecified formation of cyanoacetylene directly from HCN and the simplified treatment of propiolaldehyde production from acetate rather than from discharge chemistry on methane/water-like feedstocks.
suggestions: Specify Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox or Nieuwland-type chemistry for cyanoacetylene formation rather than HCN alone.
Model propiolaldehyde as a discharge-derived feedstock with appropriate atmospheric precursors instead of a single-step conversion from acetate.
Include isomeric cyanopyridine competition as a literature-noted selectivity challenge.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the cyanosulfidic sugar network feeding into the Trapp ribosylamine branch. The literature supports glycolaldehyde/glyceraldehyde production and borate-stabilized sugar chemistry, and the coupling of 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde with ribosylamine is a key validated advance. However, the stated endpoint 'to nicotinamide' is somewhat compressed because rxn_016 is described as one-pot formation of nicotinamide riboside from mol_013 and ribosylamine, without explicitly representing nicotinonitrile riboside and hydrolysis to nicotinamide riboside before downstream fate. Target formation is only indirect unless hydrolysis/decomposition logic is clarified.
suggestions: Explicitly include nicotinonitrile riboside as an intermediate to better match Bechtel/Kurrle/Trapp 2024.
Clarify whether nicotinamide is obtained by hydrolysis of nicotinamide riboside or by parallel direct hydrolysis of nicotinonitrile.
State mild conditions preserving the glycosidic bond, given known instability.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Kim-Benner ribosylation step is strongly literature-supported and properly centered on ribose-1,2-cyclic phosphate. However, the second step is not literature-aligned: nicotinamide riboside does not simply undergo generic surface phosphorylation to NMN under the known prebiotic route. Bechtel/Kurrle/Trapp used ITO-P-mediated regioselective 5'-phosphorylation under mild aqueous conditions, whereas the network treats phosphorylation too generically and from the wrong substrate branch. As a result, this pathway is not a coherent standalone route to the target molecule.
suggestions: Replace generic phosphorylation with explicit ITO-P-mediated 5'-phosphorylation at pH 6 and 23 C over days.
Distinguish nicotinamide riboside 2'-phosphate from biologically relevant NMN; they are not interchangeable.
Frame this branch as a stabilizing/ribosylation branch rather than a direct target-forming route.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway captures the important idea that the dialdehyde hub can feed nucleotide assembly, consistent with the 2024 Trapp work. The major weakness is again the incorrect or underspecified phosphorylation logic leading to NMN. The pyrophosphate coupling concept is plausible and literature-based, but it depends on a properly formed NMN and a defined activated AMP donor under UV. Thus the branch is conceptually sound but under-realized in reaction detail.
suggestions: Use explicit ITO-P as reagent/mineral-chemical activator in the NMN-forming step.
Specify 278 nm UV and ITO-AMP activation chemistry in the NAD+ coupling step.
Add nicotinamide riboside hydrolysis/stability constraints noted in the literature.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a chemically meaningful full-network branch because it reaches a functional redox cofactor and then hydrothermal reduction to 1,4-NADH. The reduction concept is literature-supported, but the network misses the crucial Ni-Fe alloy specificity and the fact that NAD+ reduction is favored whereas NMN is overreduced. The NMN formation issue remains the main bottleneck. Still, the environmental progression from surface assembly to hydrothermal redox activation is excellent.
suggestions: Specify Ni-Fe alloy/awaruite-like catalysts, pH 8.5, 5 bar H2, and 40 C for NAD+ reduction.
Note that free nicotinamide is redox-inactive and that the NAD+ stage is functionally important.
Repair the upstream NMN step to make the full pathway literature-faithful.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This branch aligns well with the Cleaves-Miller nonenzymatic pathway from aspartate plus DHAP to quinolinic acid and then nicotinic acid. The problematic element is the upstream abstraction of aspartate formation from pyruvate, ammonia, and CO2 in a single biochemical step; that is too compressed and not directly literature-matched. Once aspartate and DHAP are available, the pathway is strong.
suggestions: Expand aspartate formation into oxaloacetate generation plus transamination/reductive amination steps.
Specify phosphate-enhanced conditions for the DHAP-dependent quinolinate chemistry.
Clarify that this pathway reaches nicotinic acid, not nicotinamide, unless amidation/hydrolysis balance is added.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The key endpoint reaction, asparagine plus DHAP giving nicotinamide, is directly supported by Cleaves-Miller and is one of the strongest literature-congruent features in the whole network. The weakness is the feeder chemistry: asparagine generation from aspartate and ammonia is oversimplified and not clearly prebiotically validated under the stated conditions, and DHAP generation from glyceraldehyde is also schematic. So the core is strong but the full branch is only moderately supported.
suggestions: Treat asparagine as an available pool from broader Strecker/amidation chemistry if direct amidation is uncertain.
Clarify the route to DHAP or broaden to phosphorylated triose pool rather than a single exact molecule.
Retain the asparagine + DHAP core because it is one of the best-supported target-forming steps.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This hybrid pathway does a good job of showing hydrothermal carbon fixation products feeding a surface heterocycle-forming route. Network-level environmental interconnection is strong here. The main issue is that acetate-to-propiolaldehyde remains weakly justified relative to the literature, which favors discharge production of propiolaldehyde. Nonetheless, the route is connected and reaches the target cleanly.
suggestions: Use hydrothermal products as indirect carbon reservoir support rather than claiming direct acetate-to-propiolaldehyde conversion unless stronger justification is provided.
Add explicit atmospheric/discharge feeder chemistry for propiolaldehyde.
Preserve the hydrothermal-to-surface handoff, which is a strength of the network.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the best-presented convergence pathway in the network. It correctly highlights 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde as the branch-point feeding both nicotinamide and nicotinic acid through nicotinonitrile. It is strongly aligned with the core Orgel-derived literature. Only the propiolaldehyde sourcing remains somewhat schematic.
suggestions: Strengthen the provenance of propiolaldehyde using the original discharge literature.
Mention hydrolysis competition between partial and complete conversion to amide vs acid.
Optionally include isomer competition to reflect prebiotic regioselectivity limits.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway properly captures the dual convergence of nicotinic acid and nicotinamide as a vitamin B3 reservoir system. It is well aligned with literature emphasizing both the quinolinate route and amide/acid interrelationship. Environmental progression is slightly less explicit than in the strongest hybrid branches, but chemically it is robust.
suggestions: Clarify whether nicotinic acid is a reservoir that later amidates or simply a parallel endpoint.
Tie this branch more explicitly to meteoritic delivery/extraterrestrial evidence if intended as a reservoir model.

evaluation.network_feedback: The network is strong in topology and literature awareness, and it captures the two major experimentally supported prebiotic routes to nicotinamide: the Orgel/Chichibabin nicotinonitrile route and the Cleaves-Miller aspartate/asparagine plus DHAP route. It also commendably includes downstream NAD+ assembly and hydrothermal NAD+ reduction, reflecting current thinking that functional redox chemistry requires nucleotide-level products. Hub structure is good, especially around HCN, cyanoacetaldehyde, 2-cyanoglutaconic dialdehyde, nicotinonitrile, and aspartate/asparagine. Convergence is real and the graph appears connected without isolated subgraphs. The main reasons the network falls below approval threshold are chemical detail issues in several feeder and assembly steps: cyanoacetylene generation is oversimplified, acetate-to-propiolaldehyde conversion is weakly justified, aspartate/asparagine feeder chemistry is compressed, and most importantly the nicotinamide riboside to NMN logic is not faithful to the literature distinction between nicotinamide riboside, nicotinamide riboside 2'-phosphate, and ITO-P-mediated 5'-phosphorylation to NMN. The NAD+ reduction step should also specify Ni-Fe alloy conditions from the cited hydrothermal literature. Literature alignment is good at the pathway-concept level but only moderate at the exact reaction-detail level. Because approval requires >=0.9 overall confidence, this network should not be approved in its current form, though it is close and can likely be repaired with a few targeted revisions.
